coredata.dc:title: One step synthesis of tryptophan-isatin carbon nano dots and bio-applications as multifunctional nanoplatforms
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Tok, Kerem
Barlas, F. Baris
Bayır, Ece
Şenışık, Ahmet Murat
Zihnioglu, Figen
Timur, Suna
coredata.dc:description: The development of natural molecule-derived carbon nano dots (CNDs) marks a significant advancement in biocompatible and sustainable nanomaterials. Tryptophan, capable of crossing the blood-brain barrier (BBB), serves as a precursor to numerous pharmacologically active compounds, while isatin and its derivatives have demonstrated anti-tumor effects, including against brain cancers. This study aimed to synthesize fluorescent CNDs from tryptophan-isatin hybrid precursor and explore their applications in glioblastoma treatment. These CNDs were characterized using techniques such as TEM, SEM-EDS, FTIR, XPS, Raman spectroscopy and UV-Vis spectrophotometry. In vitro tests using the U-87 glioblastoma cell line evaluated cell viability, affinity, and BBB permeability. The CNDs, between 4 and 7 nm in size, exhibited blue and green fluorescence, with no cytotoxic effects observed at concentrations up to 25 µg/mL. The highest BBB permeability rate was determined as 4.3 × 10⁻⁵ cm/s. Additionally, the CNDs demonstrated radiotherapeutic properties, leading to a 51 % reduction in cell viability. This research contributes to nanomedicine by introducing a novel biocompatible material with potential for targeted brain cancer imaging and therapy, while also suggesting broader applications beyond glioblastoma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Nano Dots (CNDs)
Multifunctional nanomaterials
Tryptophan-isatin hybrid precursor
Cell Imaging
Theranostic nanoplatforms
BBB Permeability
Nanomedicine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and in vitro assays
In vitro blood-brain barrier (BBB) model – permeability analysis

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Trp-Isa hybrid in EtOH (tubes represent the ethanolic solutions, Trp alone, Trp-Isa hybrid, and Isa alone, respectively.) B) UV-Vis spectra of CNDs (tubes represent the resulted CNDs, TrpCND@EtOH, TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, and IsaCNDs@EtOH, respectively). C) Fluorescence emission spectra of TrpIsaCNDS@EtOH taken at different excitation wavelengths. D) TEM images. E) ATR-FTIR spectrum of CNDs.

An optical characterization of CNDs. A) UV-Vis spectrum of Trp-Isa samples in EtOH and B) Trp-Isa samples in water. C) Fluorescence Intensity of CNDs synthesized in EtOH (excitation at 350 nm, tubes represent the synthesized CNDs, TrpCND@EtOH, TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, and IsaCNDs@EtOH, respectively) and D) Fluorescence Intensity of CND Synthesized in water. (excitation at 330 nm).
FTIR analysis of A)Trp, B) Isa, C) TrpCNDs@EtOH, D) TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, E) TrpCNDs@Water, and F) TrpIsaCNDs@Water. Raman spectra of G) TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, H) TrpCNDs@EtOH.

Fluorescent imaging of CNDs in U-87 cell lines (scale bar ∼44 μm). (A) TrpCNDs@EtOH, (B) TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, (C) TrpCNDs@Water, (D) TrpIsaCNDs@Water. The images were generated through the overlapping of cell images treated with CNDs and those stained with DAPI.
Cytotoxicity of CNDs in U-87 cell lines. Cell viability comparison between CNDs as percentages (mean ± SD) at concentrations from 1.0 to 250 μg/mL.
BBB permeability values of each CNDs. **** p < 0.0001.
The assessment of radiosensitization involving CNDs was conducted on U-87 cell lines both with and without ionizing radiation exposure (4 Gy).
Demonstrates the synthesis steps of Trp-Isa CNDs, techniques for detailed characterization, and investigation of its potential bio-applicability on human U-87 glioblastoma cell line.

head.ce:title: One step synthesis of tryptophan-isatin carbon nano dots and bio-applications as multifunctional nanoplatforms

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Ege University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of natural molecule-derived carbon nano dots (CNDs) marks a significant advancement in biocompatible and sustainable nanomaterials. Tryptophan, capable of crossing the blood-brain barrier (BBB), serves as a precursor to numerous pharmacologically active compounds, while isatin and its derivatives have demonstrated anti-tumor effects, including against brain cancers. This study aimed to synthesize fluorescent CNDs from tryptophan-isatin hybrid precursor and explore their applications in glioblastoma treatment. These CNDs were characterized using techniques such as TEM, SEM-EDS, FTIR, XPS, Raman spectroscopy and UV-Vis spectrophotometry. In vitro tests using the U-87 glioblastoma cell line evaluated cell viability, affinity, and BBB permeability. The CNDs, between 4 and 7 nm in size, exhibited blue and green fluorescence, with no cytotoxic effects observed at concentrations up to 25 µg/mL. The highest BBB permeability rate was determined as 4.3 × 10⁻⁵ cm/s. Additionally, the CNDs demonstrated radiotherapeutic properties, leading to a 51 % reduction in cell viability. This research contributes to nanomedicine by introducing a novel biocompatible material with potential for targeted brain cancer imaging and therapy, while also suggesting broader applications beyond glioblastoma.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials present a vast array of opportunities for targeted imaging and treatments, early detection methods, and enhanced radiotherapy. Fluorescence nanoplatforms, in particular, have gained significant attention due to their unique size, optical properties, and remarkable characteristics. Among these, Carbon Nano Dots (CNDs) stand out as versatile nanoarchitectures with great potential in cancer research, exhibiting extraordinary optical properties such as strong fluorescence and high photostability, making them invaluable tools for cancer diagnostics. There are several types of synthesis methodologies for CNDs, but the microwave method is a well-established technique [1,2]. This approach involves the utilisation of microwave radiation to irradiate a solution containing the desired precursors, thereby resulting in the formation of nanoparticles. In comparison with conventional methodologies such as chemical reduction and hydrothermal synthesis, the microwave technique is increasingly favoured due to its expeditious and efficient nature [3,4]. By reducing the synthesis time from several hours to a matter of minutes, it offers a more cost-effective and scalable alternative for producing CNDs. The uniform heating provided by microwave radiation ensures better control over particle size and composition, making it a highly reliable method for CNDs synthesis [5]. The ability of CNDs to selectively target cancer cells through surface modifications and functionalization provides a multifunctional approach for targeted cell imaging and drug delivery. Additionally, their ability to sensitize cancer cells to ionizing radiation, as evidenced by previous studies, highlights their potential for synergistic anti-cancer effects [6,7]. In recent years, the synthesis of CNDs using natural molecules has gained considerable interest in nanotechnology and materials science. These natural molecule-based CNDs exhibit remarkable biocompatibility, positioning them as promising candidates for biomedical applications [8–10]. The diversity of natural precursors allows for a wide range of optical and electronic properties, enhancing the versatility of these nanomaterials [11]. Among these natural precursors, Tryptophan (Trp) is particularly noteworthy. Trp plays a crucial role in various biological processes, especially in the brain. It is transported into brain cells via the large neutral amino acid transporter 1 (LAT1), facilitating its selective uptake across the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and ensuring adequate availability for neuronal function. Trp is essential for synthesizing neurotransmitters like serotonin and melatonin, which influence mood regulation, sleep modulation, and stress responses [12]. However, pathological conditions such as brain cancer can disrupt Trp metabolism, affecting brain function and neural communication. Isatin (Isa) and its analogues have been found to enhance the efficacy of conventional cancer therapies such as chemotherapy and radiotherapy [13]. There are many studies which explore Isa and its hybrid derivatives -demonstrating their potential for a range of biological applications, including anti-tumor, apoptosis inhibitor, anticonvulsant, antiviral, antimalarial properties etc. [14,15]. Additionally, Isa has also garnered attention for its potential therapeutic properties in neurological disorders, including brain cancer. Studies have shown that Isa exhibits neuroprotective effects by modulating various signaling pathways involved in cell survival, proliferation, and apoptosis. The Trp-Isa hybridization imparts distinctive features to the resulting CNDs. The molecular interaction between Trp and Isa allows for tunable control over the optical properties of the CNDs. Indeed, the non-fluorescent compound Isa, when employed in organic solvents, initiates a strong and specific fluorogenic reaction with Trp [16]. The conjugated structures and functional groups present in Trp and Isa are crucial for affinity interactions with target cellular components particularly relevant in brain cancer research. This affinity-based approach enhances the selectivity and effectiveness of Trp-Isa CNDs in brain cell imaging and their potential for brain cancer theranostics. Here, we focus on the synthesis, characterization, and the potential bio-applications of CNDs achieved through the hybridization of Trp and Isa. The choice of these precursors is driven by their known interactions with brain-related biomolecules, emphasizing the importance of molecular specificity for potential cell imaging and therapeutic applications. The initial phase of this research involves a comprehensive characterization of the synthesized Trp-Isa CNDs using advanced techniques. Moving forward, we evaluated these CNDs in an in vitro environment, utilizing U-87 human glioblastoma cell lines. The assessment encompasses crucial parameters, including cell imaging, viability, BBB permeability, and the potential for a radio-sensitizing effect. Our findings have the potential to significantly impact the field of nanomedicine, offering new avenues for advancing not only for brain cancer cell imaging and addressing a critical gap in current therapeutic strategies.

All chemicals and reagents used in this study were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, unless mentioned otherwise. They were of high analytical grades and were used as obtained. All the experimental steps including synthesis, characterization and in vitro assessments are summarized in Scheme 1.

150 mg of Trp was dissolved in 1.5 mL of 0.5 M NaOH, and 8.5 mL of EtOH was then added bringing the final volume to 10 mL. In order to prepare Trp-Isa hybrid, Isa (dissolved in 1:9 acetone/EtOH and acetone/water) was added to a final concentration of 50 µg/mL to the aforementioned solution, and the solution was then left to incubate for 30 min. at room temperature. Same reaction media was prepared by Milli-Q water except EtOH. Following the incubation period, the formation of Trp-Isa hybrid was confirmed by fluorescence spectrometry.

For the synthesis of CNDs, 5.0 mL of non-hybrid or hybrid solution was transferred to a 25 mL beaker. The beaker was placed in a Petri dish and subjected to a 5 min incubation period in a domestic microwave at 700 W [17]. The resulting pellet was then dissolved in 5.0 mL of Milli-Q water. To eliminate any residual Trp, Isa and sodium from the solution, a PD-10 column was employed for purification. Fractions exhibiting fluorescent properties were collected, combined, and subjected to lyophilization. The obtained CNDs were then named as TrpCNDs@EtOH, TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, IsaCNDs@EtOH, TrpCNDs@Water, TrpIsaCNDs@Water, IsaCNDs@Water, respectively.

The CNDs underwent comprehensive biophysical characterization employing a range of techniques outlined below. First, the Ultraviolet−visible spectral properties were analyzed using a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 UV/VIS Spectrometer. Subsequently, the hydrodynamic size and distribution of the CNDs were assessed through Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano. Solid CNDs samples were subjected to Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) scanning from 4000 to 400 cm⁻¹ and Raman Spectroscopy sacnning from 1650 to 500 cm−1 (Raman analysis was conducted at the Izmir Institute of Technology Integrated Research Center, InVia 5K8x29 instrument). Morphological and structural investigations were conducted using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM analysis was conducted by Dicle University Science and Technology Application and Research Center, Jeol-JEM-1010), and Scanning Electron Microscopy equipped with Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (SEM-EDS, Thermo Scientific Apreo S). Surface chemistry analysis was performed with X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The fluorescence properties of the CNDs, as well as the intracellular uptake, were studied using a fluorescence microscope (Leica, DMI4000B) equipped with DAPI and red filters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in vitro assays
ce:section.ce:para: Human glioblastoma cell lines (U-87) were procured from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The cells were cultured in media such as Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) (Gibco) and Eagle’s Minimum Essential Medium (EMEM) (Gibco). Culturing were carried out in a single layer at 37°C under 5 % CO2, and passaging occurred twice within a week. The culture medium contained 1 % penicillin/streptomycin and 10 % fetal bovine serum (Gibco). This process ensured optimal growth conditions and maintain cellular integrity.

Evaluation of cell viability was performed with MTT assay [18]. Briefly, U-87 cells at a density of 8000 cells per well in a 96-well plate, followed by a 48 h incubation under standard cell culture conditions. Post-incubation, the cell culture medium was replaced with varying concentrations of the test substance. After 24 h of exposure, the medium containing the test substance was aspirated, and 110 μL of MTT solution (5.0 mg/mL in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), 10 % v/v) was added to each well, followed by a 4 h incubation. Formazan crystals were dissolved by introducing 100 μL of SDS (1 % w/v in 0.01 M PBS, pH 7.4) to each well, with an additional 24 h incubation period. Subsequently, absorbance of the formazan product at 570 nm and 630 nm, and the results were calculated by comparison with the untreated group as control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro blood-brain barrier (BBB) model – permeability analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Human cerebral microvascular endothelial cells (HBEC-5i, CRL-3245, ATCC, USA) and human astrocyte cells (ScienCell Research Laboratories, USA) were used to obtain in vitro BBB model. Astrocytes were seeded at 5 × 105 cells/cm2 concentration on underside of the PET membrane transwell inserts, and cultivated for 24 h at 37°C and 5 % CO2. After the incubation, HBEC-5i cells were seeded at 1.0 × 106 cells/cm2 concentration on upside of the inserts. BBB models were incubated 10 days and cell culture media was changed every other day. As detailed in previous study, Lucifer Yellow (LY) permeability analysis was performed for the characterization of the BBB models, demonstrating the suitability of the models for permeability testing [19]. TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, TrpIsaCNDs@Water, were dissolved in Ringer’s HEPES at 1.0 mg/mL concentration then added in apical side, and Ringer’s HEPES was added basolateral side of the BBB models. Samples taken from the basolateral side 30 min later were measured at 460 nm. The permeability values were calculated according to formulae 1 (where P is the permeability value, V is the volume of the liquid in the basolateral part, A is the cross-sectional surface area of the membrane, and [C] is the concentration), and the log BB values were calculated according to formulae 2 to determine whether compounds BBB permeable or not. The permeability analysis of each compound was conducted in triplicate. The statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism v 8.1.0 software. Statistical significance was determined using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) test with a 95 % confidence interval. Multiple comparisons were conducted using the Tukey post-hoc method.
(1)

cm
s
) =

)

]

U-87 cell lines utilized in this study are adhesive monolayer cultures that proliferate through surface attachment. In this approach, a specific 8000 cells are seeded into circular wells and incubated for 48 h under standard cell culture conditions. Following the removal of the culture medium, non-toxic concentrations of CNDs are added to each well. After 2 h incubation, the wells are washed three times with PBS, and the cell nuclei are stained with DAPI solution for 15 min. Subsequently, the cells are washed three times with PBS, and using a fluorescence microscope equipped with DAPI and red filters (Leica, DMI4000B), images are captured. The obtained images are merged using the ImageJ program, and the results are compared with control groups [20].

U-87 cells were seeded at a density of 4000 cells per well in 96-well microplates and incubated overnight under standard conditions (37°C, 5 % CO2, and 95 % humidity). Next day, the medium was replaced, and the cells were treated with the designated samples for a 2 h period. Following this treatment, the cells were exposed to a 4 Gy dose of ionizing radiation using the Elekta Versa HD linear accelerator device, following the protocol outlined by [21]. Post-irradiation, the cells were incubated for 72 h under suitable conditions, and cell viability was evaluated using the MTT assay.

Each test will be conducted a minimum of three times, and statistical analyses will be performed using t-test to compare data between test groups. P values below 0.05 will be considered statistically significant.

Fluorescent Trp-Isa CNDs were synthesized by a simple microwave assisted method as mentioned before and then, characterized by various techniques. The CNDs were synthesized using either Trp or Isa, and Trp-Isa hybrid with two different solvents (ethanol and an aqueous medium). It was observed that CNDs did not form in the reaction environments containing only Isa itself as the precursor. Therefore, the resulted samples named as TrpCNDs@EtOH, TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, TrpCNDs@Water, and TrpIsaCNDs@Water were used for further assessments.

Prior to the synthesis of CNDs, formation of Trp-Isa hybrid in EtOH has been confirmed by fluorescence measurement, and the fluorogenic properties are clearly visible using UV lamp ( Fig. 1. A). After the synthesis of TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH that exhibits similarity when compared to Trp and Isa in the same media, were given in Fig. 1. B. All synthesized CNDs based on Trp, Isa, and Trp-Isa hybrid, and their corresponding spectrofluorometric properties were presented in Fig. 2. The characteristic peak at 280 nm was due to the presence of Trp as precursor. The formation of Schiff bases between Trp and Isa was observed in UV-Vis spectra and was subsequently confirmed by detailed FTIR, Raman and XPS analysis. The average hydrodynamic diameter of TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH was found around 103 ± 6.0 nm with a PDI of 0.28, and zeta potential (mV) of around 19 ± 5.7 in DLS analysis. It has the lowest average hydrodynamic diameter compared to others (Table S1). In our initial SEM analysis, we found the particle size of CNDs to be 18–20 nm, which could have resulted from a degree of aggregation during sample preparation (Figure S1) [22]. Following, we conducted TEM analysis to obtain more accurate measurements. The TEM results confirmed that the synthesized CNDs have particle sizes between 4 and 7 nm, supporting the claim of successful CND synthesis (Fig. 1. D). The size and morphology of other synthesized CNDs were observed closely (Figure S1).

Next, the fluorescence properties of dots were analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy following emission spectra was generated from 290 to 350 nm at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1. C). It was observed that emission wavelengths were varied at different excitation wavelength, and it was also observed that the CNDs have been emitting blue and green fluorescence where shining green-colored dots were seen as shown in Fig. 4. Then, ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was employed, and the resulting spectrum unveiled distinctive peaks associated with key chemical features. Furthermore, the existing auxiliary website, FTIR Functional Group Database Table with Search – InstaNANO, was utilised for FTIR characterisation, and literature was consulted for the interpretation of the spectra (https://instanano.com/all/characterization/ftir/ftir-functional-group-search/). Notably, a sharp peak at 3392 cm⁻¹ indicated N-H stretching related to aliphatic primary amines. Additionally, a pronounced band at 3048 cm⁻¹ suggested the presence of alkene, while the peak at 1570 cm⁻¹ signified CC stretching characteristic of cyclic alkenes (Fig. 1. E). The peak observed at 3000–2800 cm⁻¹ indicates the formation of a Schiff base, The spectra demonstrated that TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH comprise a Schiff base, whereas the TrpIsaCNDs@Water sample did not represent [23]. The comparative spectrum of the FTIR analysis is shown in Fig. 3. The Raman spectra confirm that the synthesis using Trp and Isa as precursors leads to structural differences in the resulting CNDs (Fig. 3). The Raman spectra provided for TrpCNDs@EtOH and TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH exhibit key differences. The D-band represents disordered carbon or defects in the carbon lattice, which may arise from heteroatom doping (e.g., N or O incorporation) or surface functionalization. The G-band corresponds to sp²-hybridized graphitic carbon, indicating the presence of graphitic domains within the carbon structure. The intensity ratio of the D-band to G-band (I_D/I_G) provides insights into the degree of disorder in the carbon structure. Based on the relative intensities, TrpIsaCNDs exhibit a higher D-band intensity compared to TrpCNDs, suggesting increased disorder, likely due to the incorporation of Isa into the precursor mixture. This increased disorder may enhance surface functionality and reactivity. The fingerprint region displays several features unique to each sample. For TrpIsaCNDs, additional peaks emerge around 600–800 cm⁻¹, which can be attributed to vibrational modes introduced by the isatin precursor, such as C–O stretching or C–N vibrations. In contrast, TrpCNDs show relatively fewer features in this region, indicative of simpler structural components dominated by Trp. Both spectra exhibit weak signals in the range of 2800–3200 cm⁻¹, corresponding to C–H stretching vibrations. These peaks suggest the presence of aliphatic or aromatic groups in both samples. The higher intensity of these peaks in TrpIsaCNDs might be due to additional functional groups contributed by isatin. The TrpIsaCNDs spectrum exhibits distinctive peaks near 1700–1800 cm⁻¹ , which could correspond to CO stretching vibrations from carbonyl groups introduced by isatin. Such carbonyl groups are absent or significantly less pronounced in TrpCNDs, highlighting the influence of the dual-precursor synthesis approach.

The binding energies and corresponding chemical compounds identified through XPS analysis are summarized in Figure S2 and Figure S3. C 1 s scans exhibited a binding energy of 284.17, 288.13, 285.09 eV, which are represent to CC, CO/O-CO, O-H/C-N, respectively [24,25]. N 1 s scans exhibited BE of 399.36, 401.09 eV, which are attributed to NH2, OC-N, respectively [26]. Deconvoluted O 1 s scans showed at 530.8 eV implied the O-H, at 531.33 and 535.41 eV corresponds to CO/O-H, CO, OC-O, respectively [27–29].

The results indicate the presence of multiple functional groups including ketones, carboxyl, hydroxyls, and an organic matrix comprising multiple rings. Furthermore, the results of the FTIR and XPS analyses indicated that the Trp and Isa forms the Schiff base in an ethanolic media. These functional groups are postulated to originate from both Trp, Isa, Trp-Isa hybrid as observed on the surface of the TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH. These functional groups could make TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH more suitable to recognition for targeted receptors and also relevant proteins for biological approaches.

In the field of cancer therapy research, a comprehensive understanding of the dynamic interactions between treatments and cells, the extent of cellular uptake, and the precise intracellular localization of therapeutic agents is essential for the development of effective treatment modalities. Within this framework, the present investigation leveraged CNDs endowed with fluorescent properties to observe their uptake by U-87 cell lines during a 2 h incubation period, utilizing non-toxic doses (5.0 µg/mL), (
Fig. 5). The results indicate that the CNDs demonstrate superior intracellular penetration compared to their free counterparts. Furthermore, a noticeable contrast in fluorescence levels is apparent among the cells. In contrast to earlier studies where CNDs predominantly accumulated in the cytoplasm or nucleus [30,31], our findings reveal a significant localization in the cytoplasm, coupled with notable aggregation in the nucleus. It is postulated that upon entering the cells, CNDs could initially bind to cytoplasmic components, with subsequent decomposition events guiding the CNDs toward the nucleus. This observation assumes heightened significance in the context of subsequent radiation treatment, as it is believed to facilitate DNA fragmentation. A study showed that Isa reduced cell viability and induced DNA fragmentation along with chromatin condensation, indicating the initiation of apoptosis [32]. The role of CNDs in cellular uptake is pivotal, particularly intriguing in context of the affinity interactions between Trp-Isa hybrid and cellular components in the U-87 cell line. The cell-penetrating ability raises the prospect of utilizing Trp and Isa to enhance the cellular uptake. Trp crosses the BBB primarily via the LAT1 transporter and is subsequently distributed within brain cells through specific amino acid transport systems, such as System L and System A transporters [33]. Also, Trp is a precursor to serotonin involved in regulating mood, sleep, and cognition which these biochemical pathways highlight Trp inherent connection to brain function. Additionally, Trp regulates the kynurenine pathway, which produces kynurenic acid and quinolinic acid metabolites. Kynurenic acid has neuroprotective properties, whereas quinolinic acid is neurotoxic [34]. Trp interacts with various tryptophan-binding proteins within the brain. These include albumin, serotonin receptors, serotonin transporter and transthyretin in the cerebrospinal fluid, which can transport and regulate the availability of Trp to brain cells. Also, Isa is a biologically active molecule with notable implications in brain function and potential therapeutic applications. Isa has been implicated in various neural processes due to its interaction with proteins/enzymes involved into energy generation and carbohydrate metabolism, cytoskeleton formation and exocytosis, regulation of gene expression, protein folding, and cell differentiation, cell signaling, antioxidant enzyme [35]. A study showed that real-time microimaging demonstrated a broad distribution of isatin binding throughout the rat brain, suggesting that isatin binding patterns are similar to those of certain well-known receptors with significant roles in brain function [36]. The most common interaction of Isa and its derivatives is to inhibit monoamine oxidase (MAO) that degrades neurotransmitters such as serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine. This inhibition can lead to increased levels of these neurotransmitters, which are crucial for mood regulation and cognitive function [37]. Additionally, Isa binds to and modulates the activity of indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO) involved in the kynurenine pathway. By affecting this pathway, Isa can influence the production of neuroactive metabolites [38]. Isa also interacts with proteins involved in cellular stress responses, such as heat shock proteins (HSPs), which protect cells from damage and maintain protein homeostasis [39]. By modulating MAO and IDO activities, as well as influencing cellular stress responses, Isa holds promise for treating conditions such as depression, neurodegenerative diseases, and brain cancer, highlighting its potential as a multifunctional therapeutic agent in neurology and oncology. Moreover, Medvedev et al., showed that in situ experiments demonstrated that natriuretic peptides, ANP and CNP, displaced Isa from its binding sites with IC50 values comparable to those observed for ANP binding inhibition on brain membranes. This finding corroborates the functional significance of isatin's effect on natriuretic peptide receptor (NPRs) [40]. By capitalizing on these affinities, novel therapeutic strategies can be devised to traverse the blood-brain barrier with greater efficacy, thereby enhancing the precision of treatments for neurological conditions, including brain cancer. Exogenous administration of Isa has been demonstrated to facilitate the crossing of this compound across the BBB. In a study conducted on rats, the administration of Isa at a dose range of 50–100 mg/kg resulted in an increase in the level of brain Isa up to 9 mg/g [41]. Moreover, Isa and its derivatives have been shown to be effective in the potential treatment of not only brain cancer but also a range of other types of cancer, and the ongoing research into these compounds reflects their versatility and relevance in the development of novel cancer therapeutics [14,15].

To evaluate the efficacy of fluorescent CNDs as a potential biomolecular imaging agent, we first determined the cellular uptake ability in U-87 cells using a fluorescence microscope. The blue and green fluorescence observed in the cytoplasm of cells treated with CNDs confirmed the successful uptake and accumulation of the substance within the intracellular environment (Fig. 4). These results providing insights into cellular uptake mechanisms and offering valuable information for the development of novel drug delivery strategies. Even all CNDs have capability to entrance through the U-87 cells, only TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH were seen accumulated in specific region inside of the cell (Fig. 4. B). It is believed that there are some specific proteins or cellular components which Isa shows strong binding affinity. Indeed, affinity-based profiling of Isa-binding proteins in mouse and rat brains led to the reliable identification of approximately 90 unique proteins. To validate these interactions, an optical biosensor analysis was conducted using a Biacore 3000 with 5-aminocaproyl-isatin and 5-aminoisatin as affinity ligands, confirming the binding of certain identified proteins to Isa [35]. However, counterparts were seen as a spread homogenously inside of the cell instead of orientated somewhere. This unique ability of the Trp-Isa CNDs toward glowing up the cell cytoplasm further confirmed their ability as a biomolecular imaging agent.

Cytotoxicity studies based on the MTT assay were next carried out to assess the biocompatibility of the dots. Results showed that the cells-maintained viability in the presence of varied concentrations of the CNDs even after 24 h of treatment, demonstrating that CNDs exhibited no cytotoxicity toward the cultured glioblastoma cells (Fig. 5). Elevated concentrations of CNDs ranging from 1.0 to 250 µg/mL were added to cultured U-87 cells followed by determining their cell viability using the MTT assay after different incubation periods. CNDs exhibited toxic effects on the U-87 cells at 25 µg/mL concentrations as evaluated by the MTT assay.

It was observed that the cell viability of U-87 cells decreased to 60 %, when being treated with 25 µg/mL of TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH for a period of 24 h. Further, it was also observed that the cell viability of U-87 cells declined to approximately 31 %, when being treated with 250 µg/mL TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH for a period of 24 h, suggesting a dose dependent response. Thus, cellular uptake and cell viability studies in glioblastoma cells indicated that the inherently fluorescent nanoparticles could be used as potent imaging and combined therapy.

The IC50 values obtained from the MTT assay for the tested CNDs were as follows: 62.68 µg/mL for TrpCNDs@EtOH, 57.23 µg/mL for TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, 94.01 µg/mL for TrpCNDs@Water, and 92.33 µg/mL for TrpIsaCNDs@Water. Lower IC50 values indicate greater potency of the CNDs in inducing cytotoxic effects on the U-87 cell lines. Thus, our findings suggest that the TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH exhibited the most potent anti-proliferative activity. The reason why cytotoxic effect of TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH is better than others linked to diverse effects of Isa. Recent study have demonstrated that Isa not only inhibits SH-SY5Y cell proliferation but also restricts their migration and invasion [42]. Studies have indicated that Isa could induce apoptosis, inhibit cell proliferation, and impede tumor growth in brain cancer cells. These effects are thought to be associated with isatin's targeting of cellular signaling pathways, disrupting vital functions in cancer cells. For instance, incubating cells with 200 μM Isa led to a decrease in the levels of cyclin D1, MAO-A, HIF-1α, and CXCR4 proteins [42]. Additionally, the ability of Isa derivatives to traverse the blood-brain barrier and selectively target tumor cells further supports the potential role of this compound in brain cancer therapy [14,15,43].

The LY permeability measured in 30 min on the 10th day of incubation of BBB models was found 6 nm/s. The permeability value of LY should be < 20 nm/s to show that the tight junctions between endothelial cells were formed [44]. The process of passing compounds through the BBB models was carried out when the LY permeability was found to be suitable. The permeability values of TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH, TrpIsaCNDs@Water at 30 min were found to be 4.3 x 10−5, 3.12 x 10−5 cm/s, respectively ( Fig. 6.). According to these results, TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH is the one that passes through the BBB model fastest compared to the other. This could be due to the hybridization of Trp and Isa functional groups in the ethanolic environment. Indeed, several factors affect BBB permeability, including molecular weight, hydrogen bonding capacity, lipophilicity, and polar surface area [45]. However, previous studies have indicated that the most significant factor influencing BBB permeability is polar surface area [46]. The faster BBB permeability observed for CNDs in the ethanolic environment compared to the aqueous environment in our study is expected, likely due to their lower polar surface area.

Molecules with LogBB values > 0 are considered to pass through the barrier (BBB+), while those with values ≤ 0 are considered to poorly penetrate or not pass through the barrier (BBB-) [47,48]. The LogBB calculations for the CNDs resulted in LogBBTrpIsaCNDs@EtOH= 1.36 ± 0.037, LogBBTrpIsaCNDs@Water= 1.50 ± 0.055. A LogBB value > 0.3 indicates a greater ability of the molecules to penetrate the BBB and reach brain tissue [49]. Therefore, although TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH exhibits higher permeability than TrpIsaCNDs@Water, the LogBB values of both structures being greater than 0.3 indicates that each achieves effective penetration across the BBB. Although TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH crosses the BBB faster compared to TrpIsaCNDs@Water, the LogBB values of these two components differ. Indeed, the relationship between BBB permeability and logBB value is complex, and it is not reliable to predict whether the logBB value will be high or low based solely on a compound's BBB permeability. There are many factors that influence BBB permeability, which means that logBB values can vary depending on the CNDs’ chemical structure, interactions, and other properties [45]. Moreover, BBB permeability might offer a more suitable in vivo evaluation of BBB penetration and provides more insightful information compared to logBB [50,51].

LAT1, which is highly expressed in the placenta and the BBB, facilitates the transport of various drugs and hormones across the BBB [52]. This transporter also passes large neutral amino acids such as Phe, Leu, Ile, Trp, His, and Tyr across the BBB [53]. In the study conducted by Zheng et al., it was shown that the preferential transport of Trp and Phe via LAT-1 is higher than that of other amino acids [54]. Moreover, in a study conducted by Mintz et al., a strategy was employed in which Trp-functionalized QDs were used to cross the BBB via LAT-1 transporter-mediated endocytosis, based on the finding that Trp crosses the BBB through this pathway. The study demonstrated that Trp QDs successfully crossed the zebrafish BBB [55]. Therefore, it can be speculated that the CNDs used in this study may also cross the BBB via LAT-1 transporter-mediated endocytosis. In this context, it is highly advantageous to produce CNDs containing Trp on their surface, and this approach would eliminate the need for conjugation to receptors, which often involves low-yield reactions, thereby empowering CNDs to transport drugs into the brain more efficiently [55].

Ionizing radiation disrupts cell cycles by creating breaks in the DNA or by activating apoptosis pathways, leading cells to death. Alternatively, it can generate free radicals, causing damage to cellular components and activating apoptosis pathways. To augment the damage inflicted by radiotherapy, the use of radio sensitizing agents has been proposed [56]. In this study, cells were exposed to three different doses of radiation, and under normal conditions, they were incubated for three days. At the end of the experimental period, cell viability was assessed using the MTT method. As shown in Fig. 7, a single dose of 4 Gy radiation reduced viability to 63 % in the U-87 control cell line. Finally, pre-treatment of cells with CNDs applied 2 h before the experiment decreased cell viability to 57 % for TrpCNDs@EtOH, 49 % for TrpIsaCNDs@EtOH. The experimental results indicate that CNDs exhibit a radiosensitive effect, reducing cell viability by approximately 51 %.

This experimental design offers a platform to assess the influence of radiosensitizing agents on cell viability after exposure to ionizing radiation. Indeed, the high BBB permeability and selectivity for cancerous cells observed in this study indicate potential applications beyond brain cancer imaging, such as delivering therapeutic agents directly to brain tumors with minimal impact on healthy tissue. Additionally, the radiotherapeutic effects observed highlight these CNDs as promising radiosensitizers, which could be explored further for enhancing the efficacy of radiotherapy not only in glioblastoma but potentially in other cancers. This affinity-based approach could inspire the development of multifunctional CNDs designed for targeted imaging, therapy, and even real-time monitoring of treatment efficacy, which is highly relevant in both brain and systemic malignancies. Thus, this platform may serve as a foundation for the design of theranostic agents in various cancers. However, several limitations must be considered. The study is limited to in vitro experiments on U-87 cell lines, which may not fully replicate the complex interactions of these CNDs in organism. In vivo experiments on animal models are necessary to assess the safety, biodistribution, and overall efficacy of the CNDs. Also, further research into their interactions with cell membranes, intracellular signaling pathways, DNA damage and repair processes would provide a deeper understanding of the molecular mechanisms. This knowledge would facilitate the optimization and rational design of these CNDs.

This study introduces a novel methodology for engineering ‘Carbon Nano Dots’ through affinity-based interactions using of Trp-Isa hybrid. Trp is capable of traversing the BBB and plays a role in numerous metabolic processes as a precursor to a vast array of pharmacologically active compounds. Conversely, Isa and its derivatives have been demonstrated to possess anti-tumour properties against a range of cancers, including those of the brain. The investigation reveals that these CNDs exhibit distinctive optical, physicochemical, and biological characteristics, underscoring their applicability in the field of brain cancer cell imaging and potential therapy. Significantly, these dots manifest the capacity for selective targeting and penetration of glioblastoma cells, enhancing fluorescence imaging of glioblastoma, and sensitizing these cells to ionizing radiation, thereby leading to synergistic anti-cancer effects. While there are numerous methodologies for producing different types of CNDs by using various precursors or other strategies which include chemical conjugation/modification, our approach is basically the production of CNDs using Trp and Isa that provide significant benefit as this approach eliminates the necessity for receptor conjugation, which frequently involves low-yield reactions. This methodology supplies simple and selective targeting material production and may enhances the capacity of CNDs to transport drugs into the brain with greater efficiency. The study demonstrates the effectiveness of affinity-based CNDs in surmounting the limitations associated with non-specific approaches, thus improving the specificity and efficacy of glioblastoma imaging and potential therapy. This study significantly advances our comprehension of nanotheranostics, establishing a crucial foundation for the advancement of targeted and highly efficacious cancer therapies. The implications of our findings extend beyond brain cancer, offering potential applications across various cancer types besides neurological disorders and broadening the scope of nanomedicine in precision diagnostics and treatment modalities.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Bayır Ece: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Barlas Fırat Barıs: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. TOK Kerem: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zihnioglu Figen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Şenışık Ahmet: Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Timur Suna: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the use of ChatGPT 4o, developed by OpenAI, for its assistance in providing support during the final checking of this manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2025.114533.
